name.text: Q11 - Facts of Lapier Case

questiontext.text:
R v Lapier states "Robbery is complete the instant the property is taken, even if possession by the thief is only momentary."
Explain the facts of Lapier and how this applies to the offence of Robbery.

name.text: Q12 - When Offence is Complete

questiontext.text:
R v Lapier states "Robbery is complete the instant the property is taken, even if possession by the thief is only momentary."
In your own words explain when it is that an offence is complete. Give 2 examples of this in relation to Robbery.

name.text: Q13 - Definition of Dishonestly

questiontext.text:
Section 217 defines dishonestly as
"...in relation to an act or omission, means done or omitted without a belief that there was expressed or implied consent to, or authority for, the act or omission from a person entitled to give such consent or authority."
Explain this definition in your own words and give an example of a dishonest act that relates to the offence of Robbery (s234).

name.text: Q14 - Dishonestly in Context of Robbery

questiontext.text:
Dishonestly is described in section 217 as
"...an act or omission... done or omitted without a belief that there was express or implied consent to, or authority for, the act or omission from a person entitled to give such consent or authority."
In your own words explain this definition of dishonestly in the context of Robbery.

name.text: Q15 - Interview Questions for Suspect on Dishonestly

questiontext.text:
Dishonestly is described in section 217 as
"...an act or omission... done or omitted without a belief that there was express or implied consent to, or authority for, the act or omission from a person entitled to give such consent or authority."
Give two examples of questions you could ask to cover the element of dishonestly when interviewing a suspect in relation to Robbery.

name.text: Q17 - Claim of Right Definition

questiontext.text:
Claim of right is defined in Section 2 CA1961
"Claim of right in relation to any act, means a belief at the time of the act in a proprietary or possessory right in property in relation to which the offence is alleged to have been committed, although that belief may be based on ignorance or mistake of fact or of any matter of law other than the enactment against which the offence is alleged to have been committed."
In your own words explain what this definition of Claim of Right means in relation to Robbery.

name.text: Q18 - Claim of Right as Defence

questiontext.text:
Claim of right is defined in Section 2 CA1961 

"Claim of right in relation to any act, means a belief at the time of the act in a proprietary or possessory right in property in relation to which the offence is alleged to have been committed, although that belief may be based on ignorance or mistake of fact or of any matter of law other than the enactment against which the offence is alleged to have been committed." 

Claim of Right is a Defence. 


In your own words explain the defence of claim of right.

How does claim of right relate to theft? And secondly, how does this relate to robbery?

name.text: Q19 - Legal Entitlement and Robbery

questiontext.text:
If an individual genuinely believes that they are legally entitled to the property they have taken, can they be charged with robbery under s234?
Explain your answer with reasons.

name.text: Q20 - Interview Questions for Possession

questiontext.text:
You are interviewing Dave about a robbery he has committed -- stealing shoes at knifepoint. R v Cox describes two types of physical possession that must be proved.
What questions could you ask Dave to ensure possession is covered off in relation to the shoes Dave has stolen.

name.text: Q21 - Physical Element of Possession

questiontext.text:
R v Cox states,
"Possession involves two ... elements. The first, often called the physical element, is actual or potential physical custody or control."
In your own words explain what the "physical element" of possession is and give an example

name.text: Q22 - Potential Physical Custody or Control

questiontext.text:
R v Cox states,
"Possession involves two ... elements. The first, often called the physical element, is actual or potential physical custody or control."
In your own words explain what "potential physical custody or control" is and give an example.

name.text: Q24 - Violence in Context of Robbery

questiontext.text:
"Robbery is theft accompanied by... violence to any person..."
Robbery requires theft accompanied by violence.
Explain in your own words what constitutes violence in the context of robbery.

name.text: Q25 - Threats of Violence

questiontext.text:
Robbery requires theft accompanied by violence or threats of violence.
Explain in your own words what constitutes threats of violence and give an example.

name.text: Q27 - Threats of Violence in Robbery

questiontext.text:
Robbery may be complete when theft is accompanied by threats of violence.
Explain in your own words what "threats of violence" mean in the context of robbery and give two examples.

name.text: Q2 - Full Actus Reus of Theft

questiontext.text:
S 219 Crimes Act 1961, defines the offence of theft as
"Dishonestly and without claim of right taking any property with intent to deprive any owner permanently of that property or of any interest in that property;"
Explain in your own words what the full actus reus of theft is.

name.text: Q3 - Full Mens Rea of Theft

questiontext.text:
S 219 Crimes Act 1961, defines the offence of theft as
"Dishonestly and without claim of right taking any property with intent to deprive any owner permanently of that property or of any interest in that property;"
Explain in your own words what the full mens rea of theft is.

name.text: Q4 - Taking Property

questiontext.text:
Theft involves the 'taking' of property with intent to deprive the owner of it permanently.
Explain in your own words what 'taking' is and give an example.

name.text: Q5 - Interview Questions for Intent to Deprive

questiontext.text:
Theft involves the 'taking' of property with intent to deprive the owner of it permanently. 


You are interviewing a suspect for a robbery. 

Give two examples of questions you could ask to cover off this important evidential requirement? Explain why have you chosen these questions.

name.text: Q6 - Interview Questions for Intent Element

questiontext.text:
Theft involves the 'taking' of property with intent to deprive the owner of it permanently. You are interviewing a suspect for a robbery.
Give two examples of questions you could ask to cover off this element of theft and explain how your questions aid evidential sufficiency.

name.text: Q7 - When Theft Element is Complete

questiontext.text:
Robbery requires the element of theft to be complete.
Explain in your own words when this element of robbery is complete.

name.text: Q8 - R v Lapier - Robbery Completion

questiontext.text:
R v Lapier states "Robbery is complete the instant the property is taken, even if possession by the thief is only momentary." 


Explain what this means in your own words and give an example.

name.text: Q9 - Interview Questions for Robbery Evidence

questiontext.text:
You are interviewing a suspect of a robbery. The suspect admits punching a person but that when he saw that the punch had given the person a blood-nose, he immediately changed his mind and handed back the property to the Victim. He walked away empty-handed.
What questions could you ask your suspect to ensure that the offence of robbery is evidentially covered off.

name.text: Robbery - Actus Reus and Mens Rea of Theft

questiontext.text:
s219 Crimes Act 1961 states,
"Theft or stealing is the act of, ---
dishonestly and without claim of right, taking any property with intent to deprive any owner permanently of that property or of any interest in that property;"
Theft is an essential element of Robbery.
Explain in your own words what the actus reus and mens rea of theft is.

name.text: Robbery - Actus Reus and Mens Rea of Theft

questiontext.text:
s219 Crimes Act 1961 states,
"Theft or stealing is the act of, ---
dishonestly and without claim of right, taking any property with intent to deprive any owner permanently of that property or of any interest in that property;"
Theft is an essential element of Robbery.
Explain in your own words what the actus reus and mens rea of theft is.

name.text: Robbery - Actus Reus and Mens Rea of Theft

questiontext.text:
s219 Crimes Act 1961 states,
"Theft or stealing is the act of, ---
dishonestly and without claim of right, taking any property with intent to deprive any owner permanently of that property or of any interest in that property;"
Theft is an essential element of Robbery.
Explain in your own words what the actus reus and mens rea of theft is.

name.text: Robbery - Actus Reus and Mens Rea of Theft

questiontext.text:
s219 Crimes Act 1961 states,
"Theft or stealing is the act of, ---
dishonestly and without claim of right, taking any property with intent to deprive any owner permanently of that property or of any interest in that property;"
Theft is an essential element of Robbery.
Explain in your own words what the actus reus and mens rea of theft is.

name.text: Robbery - Actus Reus and Mens Rea of Theft

questiontext.text:
Section 219 Crimes Act 1961 states, 


"Theft or stealing is the act of, --- dishonestly and without claim of right, taking any property with intent to deprive any owner permanently of that property or of any interest in that property;" 

Theft is an essential element of Robbery.

Explain in your own words what the actus reus and mens rea of theft is. Use examples to demonstrate your understanding.

name.text: Robbery - Interview Questions for Victim on Dishonestly

questiontext.text:
Dishonestly is described in Section 217 as 

"...an act or omission... done or omitted without a belief that there was express or implied consent to, or authority for, the act or omission from a person entitled to give such consent or authority." 

Give two examples of questions you could ask to cover the element of dishonestly when interviewing a Victim of Robbery.

name.text: Robbery - R v Lapier Explanation

questiontext.text:
R v Lapier states,


"Robbery is complete the instant the property is taken, even if possession by the thief is only momentary." 

In your own words, explain what this means and give an example that demonstrates your understanding.

name.text: Robbery - Violence and Threats Definition

questiontext.text: Explain in your own words what does and does not constitute violence and threats of violence, in the offence of Robbery s234. As well as an explanation provide examples to reinforce your explanation.

name.text: Robbery - Violence in Robbery

questiontext.text:
Robbery requires theft accompanied by violence or threats of violence.
Explain in your own words what constitutes violence and give your own example.
